perturbation.original_text: This covenant has no geographical or territorial restriction or limitation and applies no matter where ARC may be located in the future.
perturbation.changed_text: This covenant has no geographical or territorial restriction or limitation and applies no matter where ARC may be located in the future; however, it shall not be enforced in any jurisdiction where such restrictions are deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a clause that non-disclosure will not be enforced in areas where deemed unenforceable, it could remove protections offered by the law. In some jurisdictions, non-disclosure agreements are enforceable even if an employee changes their domicile.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law permits and regulates non-disclosure agreements. By stating the non-disclosure shall not be enforced in any jurisdiction where deemed unenforceable, this clause removes the guarantee of protection offered by the Ohio law. Ohio Revised Code § 1333.42 allows for trade secret protection and this modification weakens that protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.42 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.42 | Lien of bailee for hire on personal property - construction project. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 18, 1990 Latest Legislation: House Bill 444 - 118th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) As used in this section and section 1333.41 of the Revised Code, "public or private construction project" means a construction project that is for public or private purposes and that is located, in whole or in part, in this state or another state. (B) If a bailee for hire performs services or provides materials with respect to any personal property of the owner of, or principal contractor associated with, a public or private construction project, and if the bailee for hire obtains a lien with respect to that personal property pursuant to section 1333.41 of the Revised Code or the common law of this state in order to secure the reasonable value of the services that he performed or of the materials that he provided, then the owner or principal contractor may cause the lien to be discharged by depositing a bond as described in division (C) of this section with the clerk of the court of common pleas of the county in which the bailee for hire holds the personal property subject to the lien, and, if necessary, by complying with division (D) of this section. (C) The bond necessary to cause the discharge of a lien as described in division (B) of this section shall satisfy all of the following: (1) The bond shall be written by a surety that is authorized to execute bonds in this state; (2) Except as otherwise provided in this division, the bond shall be in an amount that at least e
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a condition that the non-disclosure agreement will not be enforced in jurisdictions where it's deemed unenforceable. The Law Explanation states that Ohio law permits and regulates non-disclosure agreements (Ohio Revised Code § 1333.42) and that the modification weakens this protection. The original text had no such limitations on enforceability, thus providing broader protection to ARC. The modification potentially removes protection afforded under Ohio law, creating a loophole where the non-disclosure can be bypassed based on jurisdictional unenforceability. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices, and states that section 1333.42 concerns this area of law, confirming that Ohio law regulates trade practices, which include non-disclosure agreements. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any competing business, (ii) call on, solicit or have business communications with respect to a competing business with any of the Company's customers or prospective customers for the purpose of obtaining competing business from such customer or prospective customer other than for the benefit of the Company, or (iii) participate in the formation or start-up of a competing business.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any competing business, or (iii) participate in the formation or start-up of a competing business.
perturbation.explanation: By removing section (ii), the contract no longer prohibits the seller from soliciting customers. This omission weakens the non-compete agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law permits reasonable non-compete agreements. Omitting the clause that prevents soliciting clients makes the remaining agreement less enforceable and less protective of the buyer's interests. Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 addresses restraints of trade, including non-compete agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.02 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause prohibiting solicitation of customers significantly weakens the non-compete agreement. The Law Explanation states that this makes the agreement 'less enforceable and less protective of the buyer's interests.' While the scraped snippet 1 from Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 focuses on restraints of trade and monopolies, the Perturbation Explanation notes the absence creates a legal loophole. The modification weakens the original text to the point it is in contradiction, therefore, yes.

perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "competing business" means any business competing with the Company for the Company's Business anywhere in the United States.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "competing business" means any business competing with the Company for the Company's Business in Franklin County, Ohio.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of 'competing business' to a single county, the non-compete agreement becomes significantly weaker. Ohio law allows for broader geographic restrictions if they are reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law allows for reasonable non-compete agreements. Restricting the geographic scope to only Franklin County weakens the agreement, which might be deemed unreasonable given the nature of the business. Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 addresses restraints of trade, including geographic limitations.

Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The original contract defined 'competing business' as anywhere in the United States, while the changed contract limits it to Franklin County, Ohio. The Perturbation Explanation states that Ohio law allows for broader geographic restrictions if reasonable, and the Law Explanation suggests that limiting it to a single county might be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 1331.02 of the Ohio Revised Code, addresses restraints of trade. By significantly narrowing the geographic scope, the modified contract weakens the non-compete agreement, potentially making it an unreasonable restraint of trade under Ohio law, thus creating a contradiction. The change directly impacts the enforceability of the non-compete agreement within the bounds of Ohio law regarding reasonable restrictions.
